notes: DOCUMENTED TARGET — NOT YET RECORDED (driver_gap:interrupt). User cancels a long in-flight opencode turn with the interrupt key, then issues a recovery prompt that proves the parser reset. User-observable signal (scenario-meanings): ready→working→ready with state NOT sticking in working post-interrupt, plus a clean recovery arc on the SAME ses_… session. OPENCODE-SPECIFIC daemon shape (see assessment.json): unlike pi, opencode's parser maps a step-finish part with reason=interrupted directly to turn_done (parser.go:158 `case "interrupted"`; watcher.go:435 isTerminalPart accepts `interrupted`), so the interrupt itself is observable as a clean working→ready settle on the cancelled turn — daemon_capability:full. This spec therefore asserts the interrupt-driven working→ready (interrupt_done) on the same session, then a recovery ready→working→ready arc proving the parser reset. SESSION IDENTITY (opencode-specific): opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (agent.go) — session state lives in the SQLite store and the daemon observes a UUID-keyed `ses_…` session; the driver chains the recovery turn with `--session <id>` so all turns share one session record. session_birth is anchored to recording start and left UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> presession (if the process scanner emits one) cannot steal identity and cascade same_session_as failures; turn1_start (matching the FIRST working in the recording, necessarily the rollout session — the presession never enters working) establishes the session identity every later phase pins to via same_session_as. WHY NOT RECORDABLE TODAY (driver_gap:interrupt): the opencode interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) drives opencode through BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY send / wait_turn (no-op — `opencode run` blocks until the turn ends) / sleep in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch. There is NO `keys`/`interrupt` step and no way to deliver a mid-turn ESC against an `opencode run` that is already blocking on the in-flight turn — by the time control returns, the turn is over. Eliciting a step-finish reason=interrupted requires a live-REPL driver that can hold an interactive opencode TUI open and send Escape mid-turn. This spec is retained as the recordable target once the driver gains such a primitive (a developer task); see replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/user-esc-interrupt/assessment.json (driver_capability: gap:interrupt). HOW runs differ (once recorded): exact assistant wording (the cancelled essay text), per-turn token counts, how much streamed before the interrupt landed, the ses_… UUID/PID, and sub-second timing all vary (model-output variance). Stable structurally: a ready→working→ready arc on ONE ses_… session that does NOT stick in working post-interrupt, a clean recovery ready→working→ready on the same session, no waiting ever, a single process_exited, and a final ready state.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of agent launch (the ses_… store session); never enters waiting (no tool use)
First prompt (long essay) drives the ses_… session ready → working (binds the rollout session; a transient presession never enters working)
PID bound to the rollout ses_… session

User presses the interrupt key mid-turn; opencode writes a step-finish reason=interrupted, the parser maps it to turn_done, and the cancelled turn settles the SAME ses_… session working → ready — state does NOT stick in working post-interrupt, and never entered waiting
The recovery prompt drives the SAME ses_… session ready → working, proving the parser reset after the interrupt
The recovery turn completes back to ready on the same ses_… session; clean ready→working→ready arc, never entered waiting
Agent torn down once after the recovery turn → a single process_exited on the rollout session; the live state immediately before teardown is ready
